title: Renormalized entropy production for optimal transport in jump processes: Make conservative forces optimal again
authors: Andreas Dechant, Jann van der Meer
abstract: For continuous-space diffusion processes, there is a strong connection between conservative forces and entropy production. For a given time evolution of the system's state, the entropy production is minimized when the system is driven by a unique conservative force. However, this relation does not extend to jump processes on a discrete state space. In this case, the forces that minimize the entropy production are generally nonconservative, this effect is more pronounced far from equilibrium in the presence of high energy barriers. Here we show that, while conservative forces do not minimize the entropy production for a given time evolution, they are nevertheless uniquely characterized as the minimizer of a quantity we dub the renormalized entropy production. This work explores the properties this quantity shares with entropy production as well as crucial differences between them. We also discuss the conceptual and physical differences between the corresponding optimization problems in finite time. Our theoretical calculations are illustrated with explicit numerical examples.
summary: The paper identifies that standard entropy production is not minimized by conservative forces in jump processes. By proposing a 'renormalized' version of entropy production, the authors restore the principle that conservative forces are the optimal drivers for a given time evolution. This provides a more consistent thermodynamic framework for studying transport in discrete systems.
main_problem: In discrete-state Markov jump processes, the conservative forces that drive a system do not minimize the standard entropy production, breaking the fundamental connection found in continuous diffusion processes.
main_result: The authors introduce 'renormalized entropy production,' a new quantity that is uniquely minimized by conservative forces, thereby restoring the link between conservative driving and optimal transport.
method: The study uses optimal transport theory, variational calculus with Lagrange multipliers, and Kullback-Leibler divergence analysis of trajectory probabilities.
model_or_system: The model consists of Markov jump processes on a discrete state space governed by a Master Equation, where transition rates are parameterized by symmetric (activity) and skew-symmetric (thermodynamic force) components.
key_observables: Entropy production rate, renormalized entropy production, thermodynamic forces, probability currents, and dynamical activity.
important_parameters: Near-equilibrium vs. far-from-equilibrium regimes, inverse temperature (beta), and mobility (mu).
main_assumptions: The state space is connected, the process is Markovian, and microscopic reversibility (detailed balance) holds for the underlying transition structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a transport problem on an 8-site ring with a weak link; Figure 2 shows the time-evolution of occupation probabilities for optimal and near-optimal protocols; Figure 3 compares probability currents in bulk vs. link paths.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the discrepancy in jump processes, constructs the renormalized entropy production, provides numerical illustrations, explores trajectory-dependent counterparts, and discusses speed limits and path-probability interpretations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a new framework for understanding optimal driving and dissipation in discrete-state stochastic dynamics, which is essential for controlling quantum master equations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of the example for a finite-time transport process. The system consists of N = 8 sites in a ring con- figuration, with transitions between neighboring sites. The symmetric part of the transition rates is ω(i, i + 1) = ω0, ex- cept for the transition between sites 1 and 2, which occurs at the lower rate ω(1, 2) = ˜ω. The size of the orange circles indi- cates the probability in the initial and final state: Initially, at t = 0, the most likely site is site 1, with all other sites being equiprobable, while finally at t = τ, we want the system to be at site 3 with the largest probability. This requires us to transport probability from site 1 to site 3, which can be done...

FIG. 2. Results for the optimal protocol realizing the trans- port process illustrated in Fig. 1 with N = 8, ω0 = 1, ˜ω = 0.003, p0(1) = 10p0(i) for all i ̸= 1, pτ(3) = 10pτ(i) for all i ̸= 3 and τ = 2. The top panel shows the optimal time-evolution of the probability distribution minimizing the entropy production, while the bottom panel shows the one minimizing the renormalized entropy production.

FIG. 3. Probability currents corresponding to Fig. 2. The cur- rent through the bulk sites is defined as the difference of the probability current entering site 8 and the probability current exiting site 4, Jbulk t = Jt(8, 1)−Jt(3, 4), while the correspond- ing current through the link is Jlink t = Jt(2, 1) −Jt(3, 2). The top panel shows the probability flows for the protocol mini- mizing the entropy production, while the bottom panel shows the one minimizing the renormalized entropy production.

FIG. 4. Entropy production and renormalized entropy pro- duction for different optimal protocols. The top panel depicts the (renormalized) entropy production rate along the non- conservative optimal protocol that minimizes the entropy pro- duction (opt) or the conservative protocol that minimzies the renormalized entropy production (cons). The bottom panel shows the ratio of the entropy production for the conserva- tive forces minimizing the renormalized one and the minimal entropy production.
FIG. 5. Results for the optimal protocol minimizing entropy production for N = 6 sites. Other parameters are as specified in Fig. 2.
